title: Document Order and Stacking Order
description: Here we are learning the basics of document ordering. We can see what is available for us to help with managing the document. We talk about document flow order and the interplay between that and stacking order. Foreground, middle, and background are strictly defined. Integration of these concepts in the program are illustrated along with important interplay between system components. Good thing: These are accomplished semi-automatically.

captions.lineThree: Today we are will be talking about document order and stacking order.
This is very important for the program.
You can see on the screen that we are showing the keys which will change the grid size.
The keyboard increment size will be changed by using '[' or ']' from the keyboard.
I will now show how this works, so that you can see it.
I am using those keys, so you see it change.
The grid increment becomes larger or smaller.
The grid that we see will now be the increment size for movements from the keyboard.
The smaller visual grid gives the increment for mouse movements.
When I am using the mouse, it will be the small increment, and when I am using the keyboard, we will use the larger increment.
The keyboard increment is controlled by using '[' and ']'.
That is a very important point.
When parts are moved from the keyboard, they are immediately placed against the nearest grid line.
When parts are not lined up, the H J K and L keys will align the parts.
The arrow keys will also give movements using the same increment.
When I use the keys, the part is aligned immediately at the grid line.
When a larger grid is used, the result is the same.
It moves all the way to the nearest grid line in the direction for the key that I have pressed.
I like using H J K and L because it keeps my hands on the home row of the keyboard.
You might prefer to use the arrow keys.
The arrow keys are the natural movement keys on the keyboard.
We can see that we align in the direction of travel.
Now I am using the keys which change the grid size. Those are [ and ].
We are able to move things around very easily.
Now I have used the E key to bring us into a different mode which is used to move the edges.
When E is pressed the second time, the edge which is being changed is toggled.
To return from edge mode back in to move mode, we use the letter G.
While working from the keyboard G gives us move mode.
The mouse is even simpler to use. We simply click and drag parts around the screen.
The mouse is usually preferred, but there are some situations where the keyboard will be the best choice.
G gives us move mode. The visual border around the part has changed. That is how we know that we are using move mode.
The arrow keys continue to move the part, even while edge mode is enabled.
Only the H J K and L key are able to control the edges while in edge mode.
There are two keys which allow us to cycle from one part to the other.
It is the '.' and ',' keys which cycle through the parts.
I will now demonstrate cycling between the parts using the '.' and ',' keys.
Now I will move into an empty level by using the top row number keys. Each number brings us to that level.
How do we enter a new part from the keyboard when we have nothing?
From the mouse it is very easy to make a new part. We can drag across the screen and quickly define a new part.
I will now remove the demonstrated part from the screen using the 'r' key.
The part is now inside of the invisible 'RU' buffer. It can be returned by pressing 'u'.
I will now demonstrate that a new part may be entered from the keyboard using 'shift + n'.
'shift + n' makes a new empty part.
Now I will change the size of the grid using [ and ].
We can clearly see that the new part follows the size of the grid increment.
The size of the grid determines the size of the new part.
We can see, while in 'g' mode, our movements will use the grid increment as well.
I will demonstrate that the edge mode, using the 'e' key, also behaves in this way.
I will show toggling between the different edges using the 'e' key again.
Pressing 'e' a second time switches the active edges.
We will now focus on two important keys: The 'v' key (document order) and the 'z' key (stacking order).
I have built a visual representation that will help us to understand what happens when we use the 'v' key to change the document order.
We can visually see that the textual document will begin with the part which we are seeing at the top left, and moving down to the last part at the bottom right.
The 'z' key does not appear to do anything.
What happened?
Let's show with the mouse how the parts interact.
We can see that some parts are higher that others
Now we will use 'shift + z' and see what is different.
'shift + z' will make a special stacking order. Blue, grey, pink: That will be the order of stacking.
Holding shift while using the mouse will move an entire color group.
The same is true from the keyboard. Hold 'shift' and use H J K and L to move a complete group.
From the menu we can remove all stacking order, and revert to document flow order.
Now let's try using 'shift + v' to get a document ordering based on the color groups.
We can see that the document ordering will be: Grey, Pink, and then Blue.
The grey parts will be first in the textual document order. The search engine will see those parts first.
When using 'shift + z' we have a foreground, middle, and background.
We see the precise arrangement between the stacking order and the document flow order.
We therefore have the freedom to place parts visually while maintaining precision placement in both stacking and document flow orders.
To demonstrate the difference: Using 'v' will give us a global ordering, without regard for the color groups.
Again: Using 'shift + v' shows us visually what the document order becomes.
It is preferred to place written textual things into the grey color because it will appear first in the document.
Primary images should be placed in either the grey color or the pink color.
Imagery that is not important should be placed in the blue color group, which will be encountered last in terms of document ordering.
The color of a part can be changed from the menu.
We see that the part is shifted to a new location in the document order because of the new coloration.
The document flow order is semi-automatic.
'shift + v' is best for the final document, while 'v' is best for navigation while working.
The menu visibility can be toggled by using '`', backtick, at the keyboard.
The difference between 'v' and 'shift + v' will now be demonstrated visually.
To be clear, these colorations are a working representation, not applied to the finished document.
Well done. Tutorial Over.
